growth_records.cite: Samia Almoughrabie et al. Commensal Cutibacterium acnes induce epidermal lipid synthesis important for skin barrier function. Science Advances (2023). DOI:10.1126/sciadv.adg6262. [PMC10438445]
growth_records.snippet: different concentration of SCFA alone or in a mix containing 8 mM propionic acid, 0.6 mM acetic acid, 0.01 mM isovaleric acid, and 0.02 mM butyric acid ... SCFA composition of bacterial supernatants by gas chromatography-mass spectrometry.
growth_records.notes: C. acnes grown anaerobically in RCM (batch, 37 C, ~3 d); RCM composition not given in paper (medium_composition_stated=false). SECRETION: SCFA composition of C. acnes conditioned medium measured by GC-MS (Fig. 3D/E); the reconstitution mix reflecting that measured composition = 8 mM propionic, 0.6 mM acetic, 0.01 mM isovaleric, 0.02 mM butyric acid, so these are recorded as the measured secreted SCFA pool (concentrations, not biomass-normalized). NO rate in mmol/gDW/h derivable: no biomass (gDW) or precise time given, only ~1x10^7 CFU inoculum after growth. Propionic acid is the predominant volatile SCFA; loss of ~10,000 pmol/uL on lyophilization confirms it is volatile/abundant. No substrate-uptake rate quantified (C. acnes consumes TAGs -> SCFAs but not measured). No growth rate reported numerically (growth curves shown only graphically in Fig. 3F). pH left null: authors adjusted CM to pH 7 before cell assays, not stated as growth pH.

growth_records.medium: not stated (laboratory medium with fermentable carbohydrate)
growth_records.cite: Eady et al. A Honey Trap for the Treatment of Acne: Manipulating the Follicular Microenvironment to Control Propionibacterium acnes. BioMed Research International (2013). DOI:10.1155/2013/679680. [PMC3666392]
growth_records.snippet: The maximum growth rate achieved by P. acnes under optimum laboratory conditions with an abundant supply of a fermentable carbohydrate such as glucose is 0.2 h-1 equivalent to a doubling time of 3.5 h.
growth_records.notes: SECONDARY / CITED value in a review article; not measured in this paper. mu_max=0.2 h-1, doubling time 3.5 h reported in-text (ln2/0.2 = 3.47 ~ 3.5 h, consistent). No strain, medium composition, temperature, or pH given. Captured as literature growth physiology for P. acnes on glucose under optimal (fermentative/anaerobic) conditions.

growth_records.cite: Eady et al. A Honey Trap for the Treatment of Acne: Manipulating the Follicular Microenvironment to Control Propionibacterium acnes. BioMed Research International (2013). DOI:10.1155/2013/679680. [PMC3666392]
growth_records.snippet: in the presence of oxygen (20% air saturation), mu_max decreases to 0.04 h-1 corresponding to a doubling time of 17.25 h [38].
growth_records.notes: SECONDARY / CITED value (cited from reference 38 in the article); not measured in this paper. mu_max=0.04 h-1, doubling time 17.25 h (ln2/0.04 = 17.3 ~ 17.25 h, consistent). Oxygen-exposed (20% air saturation) condition, contrasts with anaerobic optimum record (mu=0.2). No strain/medium/temperature/pH given. In-vivo follicular estimates (D=0.114-3.36 h-1 from chemostat theory F/V) and benzoyl-peroxide-recovery doubling time ~18 h are CALCULATED/indirect estimates, NOT experimental measured rates, so not emitted as records.
